name: Xenon
name_origin: From the Greek 'xenos' meaning 'stranger' or 'foreigner', reflecting its rarity; named by its discoverers William Ramsay and Morris Travers in 1898.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Xenon is exceedingly rare in Earth's atmosphere, present at only about 0.087 ppm by volume (roughly 1 part in 11.5 million). It is obtained commercially by the fractional distillation of liquefied air as a byproduct of oxygen and nitrogen production.
states: trace in some natural gas wells

compounds.name: Xenon difluoride
compounds.summary: A colorless crystalline solid that was one of the first noble-gas compounds synthesized; widely used as a strong fluorinating agent and as an etchant for silicon in MEMS fabrication.

compounds.name: Xenon tetrafluoride
compounds.summary: A colorless crystalline solid produced by direct reaction of xenon with excess fluorine; first synthesized in 1962 and a milestone in noble-gas chemistry.

compounds.name: Xenon trioxide
compounds.summary: A colorless, dangerously explosive solid that is a powerful oxidizer; aqueous solutions yield xenic acid.

compounds.name: Xenon hexafluoride
compounds.summary: A colorless solid produced by reaction of xenon with excess fluorine under pressure; the strongest fluorinating agent among xenon fluorides.

compounds.name: Xenon tetroxide
compounds.summary: A yellow gas that is unstable and explosive above -36 degrees C; one of the few stable +8 oxidation state compounds.

compounds.name: Sodium perxenate
compounds.summary: A yellow crystalline salt of the perxenate anion (XeO6^4-), used as a powerful oxidizing agent in analytical chemistry.

uses: High-intensity arc lamps for cinema projectors, automotive HID headlights, and IMAX theaters
Anesthetic gas with neuroprotective properties (used in research and limited clinical practice)
Propellant in ion thrusters for satellite station-keeping and deep-space missions
Working medium in excimer lasers (XeCl, XeF) used in photolithography and medical procedures
Detector medium in dark-matter and neutrinoless double-beta-decay experiments (liquid xenon TPCs)
Calibration source and tracer in nuclear medicine (radioactive xenon-133 for lung ventilation studies)
reactivity_summary: Xenon is a colorless, odorless, dense noble gas that was long considered chemically inert. In 1962 Neil Bartlett demonstrated that xenon could form true chemical compounds, beginning the field of noble-gas chemistry. Xenon reacts directly with fluorine to give XeF2, XeF4, and XeF6, and from these many oxides, oxyfluorides, and salts have been derived. Its chemistry features oxidation states of +2, +4, +6, and +8, with the highest states stabilized by the most electronegative ligands (F and O).
isotopes_summary: Xenon has nine naturally occurring isotopes, of which seven (xenon-126, 128, 129, 130, 131, 132, 134) are stable and two (xenon-124 and xenon-136) are observationally radioactive with extraordinarily long half-lives via double beta decay. Xenon-129 is used in NMR spectroscopy and in hyperpolarized MRI of the lungs, while xenon-133 and xenon-135 are notable fission products; xenon-135 has the largest known thermal neutron capture cross section of any nuclide.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Xenon
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 97th Edition (2016-2017)
IUPAC Atomic Weights of the Elements 2021
